focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

The kitchen hit Silas before Junie did. Broth, damp burlap, onion skins, and that sweet-metal stink the Celadon phase left on every hot surface in town met him at the door as the first ferry bell struck the dark apart. Junie had three kettles going on a fire that should have had no business holding them. The branded fuel burned pretty and thin. It gave off a pale aristocratic glow that looked costly on a hotel balcony and behaved like an insult under a soup pot. She had wedged the cheapest coal she could still bully on credit under the grate.

He came in from the yard with his sleeves rolled and his hair still wet from the pump. His sign chest still squatted under Junie's lean-to where he'd dropped it after dark, iron corners wet with dawn. He had scrubbed at himself hard enough to raise a rash along his throat and still carried the city's slime in the seams of his hands. Paint used to live there, fine blue, vermilion, lampblack, the honest dirt of his trade. Now the creases held pale gold gum and the gray fuzz of dead moss roots.

Junie did not look up at him right away. She was shaving burnt mash from the underside of a kettle with the back of a spoon, counting under her breath the way she did when numbers were all that kept rage from turning theatrical.

"Three sacks of onions," she said. "Two pounds of eel ends. Half a brick of coal I had to kiss Pell's clerk for, and that was with my mouth shut. Morning."

"Morning to you too."

"Take the bread before Otis wakes and sees it."

He took the heel from the table. It was yesterday's rye, the crust gone rubbery in the damp. He ate it standing. Across the room the chalkboard tab list had been wiped and rewritten so often the wood looked furred. Half the names on it were ferry hands who used to pay on Fridays...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...called for the name again, wanting to hear it repeated as if repetition itself were a blessing. The choir obliged. The newsboys obliged. The vendors obliged because they could smell which way the crowd's hunger had turned. The word no longer needed Gideon's money or Silas's mouth. It had crossed.

On the lower quay Junie got her stove breathing darker again by force and foul language. The flame held, but sullenly, and not before the first row of paying customers had watched her fight it. The boy clerk stood off with the padlock in his fist while she kicked her day's cash box farther under the bench where no clerk could reach it without losing fingers. Gideon had kept his bargain exactly in the ugliest sense: he had not killed her kitchen. He had merely taught the city how to hurt it.

Damaris finally moved her pencil. He saw her write one line, stop, tear the page out, and crush it in her fist before starting again. Near the vendor rail, two women turned their branded nursery cups upside down because Inez had shouted at them to, while a third argued back that the new gold was prettier and ought to burn hotter. Inez did not turn around to see whether Silas watched. She kept moving, soot up one sleeve, breaking the sales pitch with practical warnings one pair of hands at a time.

Below, a boy no older than Otis tugged at his mother's skirt and asked to say the name again because it made him feel grand. She let him. By dusk the phrase would be in ferry kitchens, boarding-house grates, nursery trays, all the places where pale gold light could cheat heat and still get trusted for an hour too long. The first spoiled suppers and underboiled kettles would already be arriving under his syllables, and the pretty new color would still be sitting on stove lids when the music quit.
